citations: Feenstra, Luck, Obstfeld, Russ (2018) 'In Search of the Armington Elasticity' REStat | GTAP database elasticity parameters (macro/top-nest values, typically ~2 but half of second-nest) | Ruhl (2008) 'The international elasticity puzzle' (macro vs trade elasticity distinction)
reasoning_summary: At the top (macro) nest between imports and domestic goods, estimates tend to be substantially lower than the within-import (second-nest) elasticities. Feenstra et al. (2018) find top-nest macro elasticities often near or slightly above 1, with point estimates commonly around 1–2 for the U.S. GTAP convention often sets macro elasticity at roughly half the Armington (second-nest) value, yielding ~2. Business-cycle/IRBC literature (Backus-Kehoe-Kydland, Heathcote-Perri) uses even lower values (~0.5–1.5), while trade/CGE calibrations lean higher. I center around 1.5 with a right-skewed distribution reflecting disagreement between macro (low) and trade/CGE (higher) traditions.
